natural_expression: Every morning at 7:30, I have breakfast while using BBC Radio 3 as background music; it has become my fixed ritual to start the workday.

When squeezing onto Metro Line 1, if I'm frustrated by endless revisions to today's proposal, I listen to Philip Glass's minimalist pieces to calm down; if I'm in a good mood, I switch to City Pop and sway along with the rhythm.

music_entity: Hanon Finger Exercises / Debussy's 'Clair de Lune'
natural_expression: On Wednesday and Friday evenings, piano practice is non-negotiable: I start with half an hour of Hanon exercises to warm up, then grind away at Debussy's 'Clair de Lune.' Professor Zhang always says my technique is sufficient, but that hazy tonal quality still needs more polishing.

music_entity: Chopin's 'Nocturnes' or Self-selected Pieces for Lesson Feedback
natural_expression: Every two months, I have to visit Professor Zhang's home to 'get scolded' during my lesson feedback. Last time, when I played Chopin's 'Nocturnes,' I was criticized again for lacking emotional subtlety, so it seems I still have more reflecting to do.

music_entity: Vinyl Records from Hangzhou Local Independent Record Stores
natural_expression: On weekend afternoons, my favorite activity is diving into the independent record stores tucked away in Hangzhou's alleys. Like Susu, I hunt for a few vinyl records, listening to see if any offer ambient music inspiration for my next architectural proposal.

Whenever the weather is nice by West Lake, I take my Bluetooth speaker to sit on a bench, listening to 'Reflections in the Water' while gazing at the real lake water. That synesthetic experience greatly aids my design work.

Before heading to the conference room to present proposals to clients, I always play a segment of Ryuichi Sakamoto's 'Async'. This atmosphere helps everyone calm down and enter the spatial rhythm we aim for.

time_pattern: During high work pressure or anxiety
music_entity: Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier' or works by Philip Glass
natural_expression: Whenever rushing deadlines triggers an anxiety spike, I retreat to my study to play Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier'. Its rigorous structure and repetitive melodies instantly calm me down.

music_entity: Indie band performances at MAO Livehouse
natural_expression: Occasionally on weekend nights, I stand in the front row at MAO Livehouse to feel the bass vibrations of rock music like my cousin does. Although Lin Zihao and I often debate whether classical music is outdated, the energy of these live performances truly helps release stress.

time_pattern: When feeling nostalgic or missing family
music_entity: Joe Hisaishi's 'Summer' or childhood piano practice recordings
natural_expression: Sometimes when I miss my grandfather deeply, I dig out my childhood piano practice recordings or listen to Joe Hisaishi's 'Summer', recalling that summer when he told me the story behind 'Moon Reflected in the Second Spring'.

music_entity: Improvised piano composition / Mobile phone recording
natural_expression: When architectural inspiration strikes me in my study late at night, I immediately improvise some dissonant chords on the piano and record them on my phone to use as material for future ambient music demos.
When Susu and I are drinking in our rental apartment, she picks the vinyl records from our collection while I share the stories behind the tracks; these are our most relaxing moments.

Although Chopin is a representative of Western Romanticism, every time I play his Nocturnes, I am reminded of the chanting quality of my grandfather playing the Erhu. In fact, that Eastern beauty of linear melody shares a similar effect with Chopin's ornamentation.

The old Xinghai piano at home was bought by my mother through years of frugal saving while she worked at the textile factory. Although I now use a Yamaha U1 in my study, that Xinghai piano embodies her persistent belief that 'music can transform one's temperament,' serving as the heaviest cornerstone of my musical journey.

My cousin Lin Zihao always feels that classical music is outdated, preferring his electric guitar and rock band. However, when we tried pairing my improvised piano accompaniment with his distorted riffs, the collision between classical harmony and modern noise unexpectedly created a sense of architectural space.

Professor Zhang often criticizes my performance of Debussy's "Clair de Lune," saying I have technique in abundance but lack emotion. He remarks that I resemble an architect obsessed with structural precision, forgetting that Impressionist music demands an elusive haziness and the art of leaving space.

Although Su Su doesn't understand music theory or play any instruments, she has exquisite taste in selecting vinyl records. She often uncovers obscure City Pop gems I've never noticed, compensating for the limitations in my auditory horizon as a conservatory-trained musician.

When my partner Ah Jie and I discuss spatial acoustics solutions, Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async" serves as our common language. He often invites me to sample piano sounds for his white noise projects, allowing us to integrate this minimalist electronic texture into our architectural atmosphere designs.

Many feel Joe Hisaishi's music is too populist, but for me, his melodies conceal the same narrative quality my grandfather invoked when telling stories about "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring." Especially "Summer," which directly connects to the warmest channels of my childhood memories.

As an architect, I often compare Bach's "The Well-Tempered Clavier" to Gothic cathedrals in music. Its rigorous counterpoint and mathematical logic share the same sense of ordered beauty as the structural deductions I make when drafting blueprints, serving as my ultimate tranquilizer during moments of anxiety.

core_entity: China Musicians Association Amateur Level 10 Certificate
natural_expression: That Amateur Level 10 Certificate is not only proof of my persistence from the Hangzhou Children's Palace through high school, but also the greatest pride of my mother, Chen Xiuying, a textile worker. It bears witness to the twenty years during which we, mother and daughter, supported each other on our musical journey.

Philip Glass's minimalist melodies, characterized by constant repetition with subtle variations, closely resemble the modern architectural facade designs I am exploring. Both construct grand spatial rhythms through the infinite replication and slight mutation of a single element.

Although my grandfather, Lin Jianguo, never professionally performed "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring," the expression on his face when he told stories about Abing helped me understand the suffering and resilience behind this piece. This distinctly Chinese tragic aesthetic has profoundly influenced my understanding of emotional depth in music.

The Yamaha U1 in the corner of my study and the hi-fi speakers by the window create my private soundscape. This space serves both as a battlefield where I grind late into the night perfecting Debussy's tones, and as a sanctuary where I listen to symphonies, allowing architectural inspiration to resonate with music.

music_behavior: Playing Bach's "The Well-Tempered Clavier" or Philip Glass's minimalist works
natural_expression: When rushing through drawings until my heart races and anxiety peaks, I retreat to my study to play Bach's "The Well-Tempered Clavier." The airtight structure of its counterpoint, or the constantly repeating patterns in Philip Glass's music, acts like an architectural framework to support my scattered thoughts, instantly calming me down.

music_behavior: Listen to Chopin's Nocturnes or Rachmaninoff's slow movements
natural_expression: If my proposal gets rejected by the client and I feel particularly disheartened, I won't force myself to cheer up. Instead, I'll put on my headphones and listen alone to Chopin's Nocturnes or Rachmaninoff's slow movements, allowing myself to immerse in that grand melancholy, letting tears and music vent together.

music_behavior: Improvise on piano and record with mobile phone
natural_expression: Whenever a brilliant architectural flow suddenly flashes in my mind and I'm too excited to sit still, I rush to the piano to strike some dissonant chords at random, trying to capture the rhythm of that epiphany. I immediately record it on my phone to serve as the core motif for later ambient music demos.

music_behavior: Listen to Joe Hisaishi's 'Summer' or browse childhood recordings
natural_expression: When I miss my grandfather, Lin Jianguo, especially much, I dig out old recordings of myself practicing at the Children's Palace from my childhood, or loop Joe Hisaishi's 'Summer'. That melody always pulls me back to the summer when he played the erhu for me and told stories about 'Moon Reflected in the Second Spring'.

music_behavior: Listen to Debussy's 'Clair de Lune' to enter a flow state
natural_expression: Before starting to draw complex sectional views, I play Debussy's 'Clair de Lune' once. Its hazy timbre helps wash away distracting thoughts, allowing me to quickly enter a clear, focused flow state akin to 'reflections in water', making the pen in my hand move smoothly.

When working late alone in the office and feeling a sense of alienation unique to the city, Ryuichi Sakamoto's 'Async' is my perfect companion. Those fragmented electronic sounds and white noise don't try to comfort me; instead, they make me feel that this loneliness itself is a spatial existence possessing its own beauty.

music_behavior: Duet with cousin Lin Zihao on piano and electric guitar
natural_expression: Although my cousin Lin Zihao and I often debate the merits of classical versus rock, whenever we set aside our differences and pair my piano accompaniment with his distorted electric guitar riffs, the harmonious resonance that bridges generations and styles always moves me, reminding me of music's power to connect hearts.

music_behavior: Listening to City Pop during commute
natural_expression: On a Friday evening, squeezed into the crowds on Metro Line 1 after work, simply putting on my headphones and switching to a City Pop playlist transforms the cramped carriage into a flowing neon street. The upbeat basslines and urban-themed lyrics instantly fill me with an unparalleled sense of relaxation and coziness.

music_behavior: Feeling the bass vibration at MAO Livehouse
natural_expression: Standing in the front row at MAO Livehouse, when the drums and low-frequency bass from an indie band directly strike my chest, the physical vibration instills a deep sense of awe for the energy of sound. This primal impact is something I rarely experience in the refined setting of a classical concert hall.

music_behavior: Recalling mother Chen Xiuying buying a Xinghai piano
natural_expression: Whenever I hit a bottleneck in my design career and feel like giving up, remembering the silhouette of my mother, Chen Xiuying, saving every penny at the textile factory to buy that Xinghai upright piano fills me with a steadfast strength. It reminds me that both music and architecture require long-term persistence to truly transform one's character.

music_behavior: Listening to Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau' by West Lake
natural_expression: Sitting on a bench by West Lake, watching the real water sparkle while listening to Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau,' vision and hearing perfectly overlap in this moment. This synesthetic experience helped me deeply understand how Impressionist music shapes the spatial atmosphere of architecture, much like light and shadow.

music_behavior: Performing an original composition live at an architecture exhibition
natural_expression: Looking back at my master's graduation exhibition, I skipped the generic background music and instead performed my own piano piece, composed based on fluid lines, to accompany my architectural proposal. Seeing the spark in the eyes of the judges and the audience, that sense of achievement from the perfect fusion of architecture and music remains the most satisfying moment of my career.

music_activity: Listening to a classical music radio station while having breakfast in the morning
companion_behavior: Reviewing the day's architectural drawings or browsing design websites
context: Weekday mornings at 7:30, at the dining table at home
natural_expression: Every morning at 7:30, I have a habit of eating a simple breakfast while using BBC Radio 3 as background sound, simultaneously flipping through the day's architectural drawings. This dual input of visual and auditory stimuli helps me establish a sense of rational order before I even step into the office.

music_activity: Wearing noise-canceling headphones to listen to music during the commute
companion_behavior: Manually switching playlists based on work mood (Minimalism/City Pop)
context: During the commute on Metro Line 1
natural_expression: When squeezing onto Metro Line 1, my noise-canceling headphones serve as a barrier against the chaos. I switch playlists with the same precision as tuning architectural lighting: when I'm overwhelmed by revisions, I listen to Philip Glass's minimalism to 'noise-cancel' my stress; when I'm relaxed, I switch to City Pop to lighten my step.

music_activity: Piano practice on Wednesday and Friday evenings
companion_behavior: Strictly performing Hanon finger exercises as a warm-up before refining specific pieces
context: Home study, in front of a Yamaha U1 piano
natural_expression: My piano practice sessions on Wednesday and Friday evenings are sacrosanct. I have an almost compulsive habit: I must play Hanon finger exercises for half an hour to fully warm up my fingers before I dare to touch the delicate tones in Debussy's 'Clair de Lune'. Otherwise, I feel I would be letting down Professor Zhang's teachings.

music_activity: Playing ambient music before presenting a proposal to the client
companion_behavior: Carefully selecting tracks and adjusting Bluetooth speaker volume to match the spatial feel
natural_expression: Before presenting a proposal to the client in the conference room, I always arrive ten minutes early to carefully select a track, such as Ryuichi Sakamoto's 'Async', and meticulously adjust the Bluetooth speaker's volume. This ensures the music acts like invisible soft furnishings, guiding the meeting's rhythm into the specific spatial atmosphere we desire.

music_activity: Weekend visits to independent record stores
companion_behavior: Listening to vinyl while jotting down ambient music cues suitable for architectural inspiration
context: Local independent record stores in Hangzhou
natural_expression: When I duck into an independent record store tucked away in a Hangzhou alley on a weekend afternoon, it's not just to hunt for the vinyl records Susu recommended. I also make it a habit to carry a small notebook, immediately noting down any distinctive City Pop or minimalist tracks I hear and the architectural spaces they might suit, building a future library of inspiration.

music_activity: Listening to Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau' by West Lake
companion_behavior: Carrying a portable Bluetooth speaker and gazing at the lake surface to seek synesthesia
natural_expression: On weekends with good weather, I deliberately bring my portable Bluetooth speaker to a bench by West Lake. I play Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau' and do nothing else but stare at the shimmering real water, attempting to capture that Impressionist quality of light and shadow through the overlap of vision and sound, for use in my facade designs.

music_activity: Playing Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier' when anxious
companion_behavior: Turning off the study lights, keeping only the desk lamp, and focusing on the mechanical motion of the keys
natural_expression: Whenever anxiety peaks during a drafting rush, I turn off the main lights in my study, leaving only a desk lamp on, and play Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier' in the darkness. The ritual of focusing on the mechanical movement of my fingers under limited light, paired with the rigorous counterpoint, quickly pulls my thoughts from chaos back into order.

companion_behavior: Immediately pulling out a phone to record and verbally noting architectural associations
context: Late night, home study or office
natural_expression: Whenever I improvise an interesting dissonant chord on the piano late at night, my muscle memory drives me to pull out my phone and record it a second before the melody fades. I immediately speak into the recording to capture the architectural circulations that just flashed through my mind, fearing that this fleeting inspiration might otherwise be lost.

music_activity: Gathering with roommate Susu to listen to vinyl
companion_behavior: Sipping drinks while explaining the background stories of the tracks
natural_expression: When Susu and I drink and listen to vinyl in our rental apartment, we have a tacit division of labor: she is responsible for picking out those obscure gems from her collection, while I explain the composer gossip and historical context behind each track as we sip our drinks. This sharing of 'sound plus knowledge' is our most relaxing moment.

companion_behavior: Standing in the front row to feel the bass vibrations and observe crowd dynamics
natural_expression: When I occasionally visit MAO Livehouse to see indie bands, I never look for a seat. Instead, I insist on standing in the front row, not just to feel the bass vibrations of the rock music like my cousin Lin Zihao does, but also to observe how the crowd flows with the music. This often provides unexpected inspiration for my public space designs.

music_activity: Listening to Joe Hisaishi's "Summer" when feeling nostalgic
companion_behavior: Searching for and playing old piano practice recordings from childhood at the Children's Palace
natural_expression: When I miss my grandfather Lin Jianguo deeply, I don't just listen to Joe Hisaishi's "Summer" online; I also dig out my old piano practice recordings from my time at the Hangzhou Children's Palace stored on my hard drive. Listening to those slightly immature yet passionate notes makes me feel as if I've returned to that summer when he played the erhu and told me stories.

music_activity: Discussing spatial acoustics with colleague Ah Jie
companion_behavior: Playing piano samples on the spot for his white noise project
natural_expression: When discussing spatial acoustic solutions with Ah Jie, we don't just talk theory. I habitually sit at the piano and improvise a few segments based on the atmosphere he wants for him to sample. Watching him process these piano tones into white noise in real time, this 'performance plus technology' collaboration model makes our designs much more practical.

So now I often go to West Lake on weekends, listening to Debussy while watching the water, trying to rediscover that synesthesia where sight and sound merge. Maybe next time I'll finally satisfy him during my lesson.

Strangely, whenever I play Chopin's Nocturnes now, I feel those ornamentations hide the unique beauty of Eastern linear melody characteristic of the Erhu. Maybe this is the enlightenment my grandfather gave me.

Although this Yamaha U1 in the corner of my study has a better tone, every time I sit here to practice, I still think of that old Xinghai.
